findings.title: Calling Start twice breaks the single-executor guarantee
findings.detail: Start unconditionally launches q.run. A second Start on the same Queue creates two owner goroutines draining the same submit channel, so two submitted critical sections can execute concurrently. Both goroutines also defer close(q.done), so cancelling their contexts can panic with close of closed channel. The comment says to call Start exactly once, but the exported type does not enforce the invariant that protects the merge exclusion domain.
findings.suggested_fix: Guard Start with sync.Once or return an error on a second Start. Keep a single owner goroutine per Queue and make done closure owned by that goroutine only.

findings.title: Strict FIFO depends on unspecified channel sender ordering
findings.detail: The queue's FIFO guarantee is implemented by relying on blocked senders on an unbuffered channel being released in FIFO order. The Go language spec does not guarantee FIFO scheduling for competing channel sends, but RSM-015 requires FIFO ordering among concurrent submissions. A runtime or scheduler change could reorder merge submissions while the code and tests still assume strict arrival order.
findings.suggested_fix: Assign an explicit monotonic sequence at submission intake and have one goroutine order by that sequence, or narrow the documented guarantee to mutual exclusion if strict FIFO is not required.

findings.title: Merge-queue scaffold still allows git push inside the exclusion domain
findings.detail: commitAllowlist includes "git push", and the fixture records it inside Queue.Submit. That contradicts specs/run-state-machine.md RSM-017, which requires the network git push to run outside the merge queue exclusive section and requires conformance tests to assert no git push inside it. This stale package-level scaffold can bless the exact critical-section creep the merge queue is meant to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove git push from commitAllowlist, add an explicit forbidden-network check for it, and record push outside Submit in the test fixture.

findings.title: FIFO test relies on timing instead of proving concurrent ordering
findings.detail: TestFIFOOrderUnderConcurrentSubmits sleeps 2ms between goroutine launches to make each sender likely block before the next starts. That manufactures the order being asserted and is timing-fragile under load; it also does not exercise truly concurrent submissions, which is the case tied to the FIFO guarantee and the channel-ordering risk in Queue.New.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the sleep choreography with a deterministic queue-controlled ordering signal, or limit the test to properties the implementation actually guarantees, such as mutual exclusion and no lost or duplicated execution.
